transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Hannah, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, thanks for having me. I guess... I don’t know, I’ve been feeling a bit off lately. It’s just that sometimes I feel like I’m not really valued by Ivan’s family, especially when his mom talks to me. It’s hard to shake off those feelings, you know? But I’m not sure if I want to dive into that right now. Maybe we could talk about something else?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I appreciate you sharing that with me, Hannah. It sounds like you’re experiencing some difficult feelings related to how Ivan’s family, particularly his mom, treats you. It’s understandable to feel off when you sense a lack of value in those interactions. If you’re not ready to dive into that, that’s completely okay. What else is on your mind that you’d like to explore instead?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I guess I’ve been thinking a bit about how I handle things when I feel that way. I try to talk to Ivan about it, but sometimes it just feels like he doesn’t really get it. I don’t want to burden him with my feelings, you know? So, I end up talking to my own family, but it’s just... complicated. I’m not sure if I want to keep bringing it up. Maybe I should just deal with it on my own?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re in a tough spot, trying to navigate your feelings while also being considerate of Ivan. I hear that you’re feeling hesitant to burden him, which makes sense. It’s also clear that talking to your own family feels complicated for you. When you say you might want to deal with it on your own, what does that look like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess when I think about dealing with it on my own, it means just trying to push those feelings aside and not let them affect me too much. I might distract myself with other things, like work or spending time with friends. But deep down, I know that doesn’t really solve anything. It’s just... easier, I suppose. I don’t want to keep bringing up the same issues, especially if it feels like no one really understands. So, I just try to keep it to myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re using distractions as a way to cope, which can feel easier in the moment, but you’re also aware that it doesn’t really address the underlying feelings. It’s understandable to want to avoid bringing up the same issues, especially if you feel like they’re not being understood. When you keep these feelings to yourself, how does that impact you over time?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Honestly, it just builds up, you know? I try to keep everything together, but then I end up feeling really overwhelmed. Sometimes I feel sad or angry, and it’s like I’m carrying this weight around. I don’t want to be a burden, so I just keep it in. But then, when something small happens, it feels like it all comes crashing down. I guess I just wish I could find a way to express what I’m feeling without it being such a big deal.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really heavy to carry those feelings around, and I can see how trying to hold everything together can lead to feeling overwhelmed. It’s tough when small things trigger that buildup of emotions. You mentioned wishing you could express what you’re feeling without it being a big deal—what do you think would help you feel more comfortable sharing those feelings, even in small ways?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe if I had a way to bring it up without it feeling so intense, like just casually mentioning it or finding a moment when things are light, it might help. But I also worry about how it’ll be received. I don’t want to seem like I’m complaining or being dramatic. I guess I just need to find the right moment or maybe even a different way to say it. It’s just hard to figure out how to do that without feeling vulnerable.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that you’d want to find a more casual way to bring up your feelings, especially to avoid feeling dramatic or like you’re complaining. It sounds like you’re balancing the desire to express yourself with the fear of vulnerability and how it might be received. When you think about finding the right moment or way to say it, what kind of support or response would make you feel more at ease in that situation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think if Ivan could respond in a way that feels understanding and not dismissive, that would help a lot. Like, if he could just listen and acknowledge what I’m saying without trying to fix everything right away. Sometimes I just need someone to hear me out, you know? It would make it feel safer to share more. I guess I just want to feel like my feelings matter, even if they seem small or silly to others.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like having Ivan listen and acknowledge your feelings without jumping to fix things would create a sense of safety for you. That validation seems really important to you, especially when you’re feeling vulnerable. It’s completely valid to want your feelings to matter, no matter how small they may seem. When you think about expressing this need to Ivan, what do you think might help you communicate that to him?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe I could try to explain to him that sometimes I just need him to listen and be there for me without feeling like he has to solve anything. I could pick a calm moment to bring it up, maybe when we’re just hanging out. But I still feel a bit nervous about it. I don’t want him to think I’m asking for too much or that I’m being needy. It’s just hard to find the right words, and I worry about how he might react.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have a thoughtful approach in mind for how to communicate your needs to Ivan, and it’s understandable to feel nervous about it. Wanting to express yourself without feeling like you’re asking for too much is a common concern. It seems like finding the right words is part of what makes this feel challenging for you. What do you think might help you feel more confident in expressing this to him, even if it feels a bit vulnerable?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe if I took some time to really think about what I want to say and even practiced it a little, that could help. I could write down my thoughts or even talk it out with someone else first. That way, I wouldn’t feel so lost in the moment. I think just knowing that I have a clear idea of what I want to communicate might ease some of that nervousness. It’s just... I still worry about how he’ll take it, but I guess if I’m honest about my feelings, that’s a step in the right direction, right?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like taking the time to think through what you want to say and even practicing it could really help you feel more prepared and confident. Writing down your thoughts or talking it out with someone else can provide clarity, which might ease some of that nervousness. You’re right that being honest about your feelings is a positive step forward, even if it feels a bit daunting. How do you feel about the idea of practicing or preparing your thoughts? Does that feel manageable for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I think it does feel manageable. Just having a plan in place makes it seem less overwhelming. I like the idea of writing things down; it might help me organize my thoughts better. I guess I just need to remind myself that it’s okay to be vulnerable and that sharing my feelings doesn’t make me weak or needy. It’s just part of being in a relationship, right? So, yeah, I think I can give it a try.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s great to hear that having a plan feels manageable and that you’re open to writing things down to organize your thoughts. You’re absolutely right—being vulnerable and sharing your feelings is a natural part of being in a relationship, and it doesn’t make you weak or needy. It sounds like you’re taking a positive step toward expressing yourself, which is really important. How do you feel about moving forward with this plan? What’s your next step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think my next step would be to set aside some time to write down my thoughts and feelings. I want to be clear about what I need from Ivan and how I want to express that. Maybe I’ll find a quiet moment this week when I can focus on it without distractions. Once I have that down, I can feel more prepared to bring it up with him. I guess I just need to take it one step at a time and remind myself that it’s okay to be open about what I’m feeling.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan, and setting aside dedicated time to write down your thoughts can really help you clarify what you want to express. Taking it one step at a time is a wise approach, and it’s wonderful that you’re reminding yourself of the importance of being open about your feelings. How do you feel about the upcoming week as you prepare for this conversation with Ivan? Are there any specific feelings or thoughts you anticipate as you take these steps?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ll probably feel a mix of nervousness and hope. Nervous because it’s always a bit scary to put myself out there, but hopeful because I really want to improve how we communicate. I guess I’m also a little anxious about how he’ll react, but I’m trying to remind myself that it’s okay to express what I need. I just hope that when I do share, it leads to a better understanding between us. I want to feel more connected, so I guess that’s what keeps me motivated.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely natural to feel a mix of nervousness and hope as you prepare for this conversation. It’s clear that your desire for better communication and connection with Ivan is a strong motivator for you. Acknowledging those feelings of anxiety while also holding onto hope shows a lot of courage. As you move forward, it might be helpful to remind yourself that it’s okay to feel vulnerable, and that sharing your needs can lead to deeper understanding and connection. Is there anything else you’d like to explore or discuss as you prepare for this next step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I feel pretty good about what we’ve talked about today. It’s nice to have a clearer idea of how to approach this with Ivan. I guess I just want to make sure I stay focused on my feelings and not get too caught up in worrying about how he might respond. Maybe I could also remind myself that it’s a process, and it’s okay if it doesn’t go perfectly the first time. Just taking this step feels important to me. Thank you for helping me sort through all of this.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you feel good about our conversation today and that you have a clearer idea of how to approach this with Ivan. Staying focused on your feelings and recognizing that it’s a process is a wise perspective. It’s important to be gentle with yourself as you navigate this, and it sounds like you’re ready to take that important step. Thank you for sharing your thoughts and feelings with me. If you ever want to talk more about this or anything else, I’m here for you. Take care as you move forward!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. It feels good to have someone to talk to about all of this. I’ll definitely keep what we discussed in mind as I move forward. I might reach out again if I need to talk more. Take care, and thanks for your support!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Hannah. I’m glad to hear that our conversation has been helpful for you. Remember, it’s perfectly okay to reach out whenever you need to talk more. Take care of yourself, and I wish you the best as you move forward with your conversation with Ivan. I’m here whenever you need support!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much! I really appreciate your understanding and support. It means a lot to me. I’ll take care and keep working on expressing my feelings. I’ll definitely reach out if I need to talk again. Have a great day!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Hannah! I’m glad I could be here for you. It’s wonderful to hear that you’re committed to expressing your feelings. Take your time with it, and remember that it’s a journey. Have a great day as well, and I look forward to hearing from you whenever you’re ready to talk again. Take care!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I’ll keep that in mind. I appreciate your encouragement and understanding. I’ll take care, and I look forward to our next conversation. Have a great day!
